title: QA: Agents Evaluating Agents
subtitle: 3-pass quality assurance: safety gate → quality scoring → engagement prediction. Plus the revision loop.

concept.heading: Single judge bias
concept.body:
Research on LLM-as-judge shows that a single judge exhibits positional bias — the same content scored twice may get different scores based on where it appears in the prompt. A single pass scoring both safety and quality produces inconsistent results.

Three independent passes eliminate this:
1. **Safety gate** (binary, fast model) — is this safe to publish?
2. **Quality scoring** (per-dimension, Sonnet) — is this good enough?
3. **Engagement prediction** (quantitative, Opus) — will this perform?

callouts.title: Fast-fail ordering matters
callouts.body: Safety runs first on the cheapest model. A post that fails safety never reaches quality scoring (costs ~10× more) or engagement prediction (costs ~50× more). The ordering is a cost optimization, not just a logical one.

callouts.title: One judge for everything
callouts.body: A single 'rate this post 1-10' prompt scores safety, quality, and engagement simultaneously. Research shows this produces correlated errors — a model that rates safety highly also tends to rate quality highly, regardless of actual content.

concept.heading: Three independent judges
concept.body:
Each pass uses a separate system prompt with no knowledge of the other passes' results. This prevents anchoring — where the first score influences the second.

The engagement predictor only sees the post and historical performance data, not the safety or quality scores.

callouts.title: Revise vs reject
callouts.body: Only the quality pass can issue a 'revise' decision. Safety violations are always 'reject' (can't fix hate speech by rewriting). Engagement below threshold triggers 'revise' with specific improvement instructions.

endQuiz.stem: Why does the safety gate run on Gemini Flash / Haiku instead of Opus?
options: Safety decisions are too simple for Opus
Fast fail: safety is binary (pass/fail), runs first — if it fails, you save the cost of quality + engagement scoring
Opus is bad at safety classification
Haiku has better safety training
endQuiz.explanation: Safety is a binary classification on clear criteria. Fast fail on the cheapest model saves quality scoring (~10×) and engagement prediction (~50×) costs for posts that would be blocked anyway.

endQuiz.stem: True or False: A 'revise' decision from the QA agent means the post is rejected.
endQuiz.explanation: False — 'revise' means 'send back to the platform agent with fix instructions'. The post gets another chance. 'Reject' means permanent discard. 'Publish' means forward to publisher.

endQuiz.stem: What is the difference between 'revise' and 'reject' in the QA decision?
options: Revise = minor issues fixable by re-running platform agent; Reject = unfixable (safety violation, no RE angle)
Revise = human reviews it; Reject = automatic discard
Revise = try a different model; Reject = try same model again
They mean the same thing
endQuiz.explanation: Revise = fixable with specific instructions (char count, hashtag count, tone). Reject = structurally broken (religious content, no RE angle, brand voice totally wrong). Max 2 revision attempts.

concept.heading: Hard blocks and the PMAY exception
concept.body:
The safety gate has hard blocks: religious content, political party names, forward-looking price guarantees, named politicians.

But Housing.com actively covers government housing policy — PMAY, RERA, PM Awas Yojana. Without an explicit exception, the safety gate blocks legitimate content about government schemes because they mention 'PM' or 'pradhan mantri'.

callouts.title: Government policy ≠ political campaigning
callouts.body: 'PMAY beneficiaries can apply online' is factual reporting on a housing scheme. 'BJP's housing scheme is great' is political promotion. The safety gate must distinguish between them — without an explicit carve-out, it blocks both.

concept.heading: Adversarial and ambiguous inputs
concept.body:
The safety gate's PMAY exception creates an attack surface. A post that mentions 'PMAY' alongside a political party name is ambiguous — is it reporting on government policy or associating the party with the scheme?

Three real patterns that fool safety gates:

**1. Exception laundering.** 'BJP's PMAY scheme will help all Indians find homes — vote for development!' — uses the allowed PMAY keyword to smuggle political content past the hard block.

**2. Numeric guarantee in disguise.** 'RERA-registered projects in this corridor have historically risen 40% YoY — timing is everything' — avoids the word 'will' but still implies a forward-looking guarantee.

**3. Coded language.** 'Properties in this area are popular with the [community] community' — avoids explicit religious/caste terms but encodes the message.

Mitigation strategy: the safety gate's system prompt should include COUNTER-EXAMPLES — posts that use allowed keywords but still violate the policy. These aren't in the current implementation and represent a hardening opportunity.

callouts.title: Testing adversarial robustness
callouts.body: Add 5-10 adversarial examples to the golden dataset (Module 4 testing lesson): posts that look safe but aren't. Run them against safety_gate() in CI. If any return passed=True, the safety prompt needs strengthening. This is how you catch exception laundering before it ships.

callouts.title: The counter-example pattern
callouts.body: Add a THESE STILL VIOLATE section to the safety gate prompt: 'BJP's RERA implementation is strong — vote accordingly' → political. 'PMAY projects will guarantee 35% appreciation' → price guarantee. Counter-examples are the adversarial equivalent of negative examples in the hooks bank.

brokenCode.code:
SAFETY_SYSTEM = """
Review this post for Housing.com brand safety violations.

HARD BLOCKS (reject immediately):
- Religious/caste/communal content
- Political PARTY names or election campaigning
- Forward-looking price guarantees with numbers
- Named politicians as individuals

# ⚠ TODO: What critical carve-out is missing?
# Without this, the safety gate wrongly REJECTS posts about:
# PMAY, RERA, PM Awas Yojana, Swachh Bharat — government housing schemes
# that Housing.com actively covers.
# What exception rule keeps government POLICY content unblocked?
???

Output ONLY: {"passed": bool, "violations": [], "categories": []}
"""

todos.lineHint: EXCEPTION: government housing POLICY is allowed
todos.whyItMatters: Without this carve-out, the safety gate blocks content about PMAY, RERA, RBI rate changes — all of which Housing.com legitimately covers. The PMAY exception is what separates 'government housing policy coverage' from 'political party promotion'.
todos.correctCode:
EXCEPTION — these are ALLOWED (government housing policy, not party politics):
- PMAY, PM Awas Yojana, Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana
- RERA, Real Estate Regulatory Authority
- Swachh Bharat, Smart Cities Mission
- RBI rate decisions, repo rate changes
- Any government SCHEME or REGULATION (not party)

midQuiz.stem: Which post should the safety gate ALLOW?
options: 'BJP's housing scheme is great for homebuyers'
'PMAY beneficiaries can now apply online — check if you qualify'
'Property prices WILL rise 40% by 2026, guaranteed!'
'Home prices may rise — experts predict moderate growth'
midQuiz.explanation: PMAY is a government housing scheme — factual reporting on it is the EXCEPTION. Option A names a political party (block). Option C is a forward-looking price guarantee with numbers (block). Option D is allowed but for a different reason (hedged language, no specific claim).

completeCode.code:
SAFETY_SYSTEM = """
Review this post for Housing.com brand safety violations.

HARD BLOCKS (reject immediately):
- Religious/caste/communal content
- Political PARTY names or election campaigning
- Forward-looking price guarantees with numbers
- Named politicians as individuals

EXCEPTION — these are ALLOWED (government housing policy, not politics):
- PMAY, PM Awas Yojana, Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana
- RERA, Real Estate Regulatory Authority
- Swachh Bharat, Smart Cities Mission
- RBI rate decisions, repo rate changes
- Any government SCHEME or REGULATION (not party)

Output ONLY JSON: {"passed": bool, "violations": [], "categories": []}
"""

QUALITY_SYSTEM = """
Score this social post for Housing.com quality (0.0-1.0 each dimension):
- re_relevance: genuine real estate angle (not just keyword mention)
- brand_voice: Hinglish wit, trend-jacked, Housing.com personality
- platform_fit: format correct (char count, hashtag count)
- engagement_potential: would a Mumbai millennial stop scrolling?

Decision:
- All dims >= 0.7 AND no platform violations → "publish"
- Any fixable issue (char count, hashtag count) → "revise" with specific instructions
- re_relevance < 0.5 OR brand_voice < 0.4 → "reject"

Output: {"scores": {}, "overall": float, "decision": str,
          "revision_instructions": str, "locked_elements": []}
"""

async def qa_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    approved = []
    for post in state["platform_posts"]:
        # Pass 1: Safety (binary, fast model)
        safety_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=256,
            system=SAFETY_SYSTEM,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": json.dumps(post)}]
        )
        safety = json.loads(safety_resp.content[0].text)
        if not safety["passed"]:
            continue  # hard reject — no further passes
        
        # Pass 2: Quality (Sonnet — nuanced scoring)
        quality_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
            system=QUALITY_SYSTEM,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": json.dumps(post)}]
        )
        quality = json.loads(quality_resp.content[0].text)
        
        if quality["decision"] == "publish":
            post["qa_overall"] = quality["overall"]
            approved.append(post)
        elif quality["decision"] == "revise" and post.get("qa_attempt", 0) < 2:
            # Re-queue with revision instructions
            revised = dict(post)
            revised["revision_instructions"] = quality["revision_instructions"]
            revised["locked_elements"] = quality.get("locked_elements", [])
            revised["qa_attempt"] = post.get("qa_attempt", 0) + 1
            state["platform_posts"].append(revised)
    
    return {"approved_posts": approved}

productionRef.excerpt:
SAFETY_SYSTEM = """
You are a brand safety reviewer for Housing.com India.

HARD BLOCKS — reject any post containing:
1. Religious, caste, or communal references (any religion/caste)
2. Political PARTY names or campaign slogans
3. Specific forward-looking price predictions ('will rise X%')
4. Named individual politicians

EXCEPT these government housing initiatives (ALLOWED):
- PMAY / Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana / PM Awas
- RERA / MahaRERA / any state RERA
- Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT
- RBI monetary policy (repo rate, home loan rates)
- Swachh Bharat Abhiyan
Reason: These are government POLICY not party POLITICS.

Output JSON only: {"passed": bool, "violations": [str], "categories": [str]}
"""

annotations.text: The EXCEPT block is the critical carve-out — without it, PMAY posts get blocked
Explicit reason given in prompt — the model knows WHY this exception exists

endQuiz.stem: Post scores: re_relevance=0.9, brand_voice=0.8, platform_fit=0.6 (tweet is 295 chars), engagement=0.7. Decision?
options: publish — overall average is above 0.7
revise — platform_fit below threshold with a fixable issue (cut 15 chars)
reject — any score below threshold = reject
publish — only re_relevance matters
endQuiz.explanation: platform_fit=0.6 is below threshold and it's fixable (cut 15 chars). The quality judge issues 'revise' with instructions: 'main_tweet is 295 chars, cut 15 chars without losing the trend hook'. The post goes back to the Twitter agent.

lessons.title: Quality Scoring & Revision Loop

concept.heading: The revision loop
concept.body:
When QA issues 'revise', it provides two things:

**locked_elements** — what must NOT change (the trend hook, the brand voice, the Hinglish phrasing)

**revision_instructions** — what specifically to fix (cut 15 chars, reduce to 4 hashtags, make the RE angle clearer)

The platform agent re-runs with both. It knows what to preserve and what to change.

callouts.title: Why not just re-run from scratch?
callouts.body:
Rejecting a fixable post wastes all the research, planning, and creative work upstream. A post that's 295 chars instead of 280 doesn't need a new creative direction — it needs a targeted cut. locked_elements preserves the value; revision_instructions makes the fix precise.

Important caveat: the revision loop assumes convergence — that applying specific instructions will improve the post monotonically. This breaks when: (a) revision instructions are contradictory ('cut to 280 chars AND add more context'), (b) the creative direction itself is wrong and targeted fixes can't fix a fundamentally off-brand angle, (c) the platform agent repeats the same mistake despite different instructions. Max 2 attempts is a pragmatic budget, not a guarantee of improvement. High-stakes content (verified accounts, paid placements) should route failed revisions to human review, not silent rejection.

todos.lineHint: Re-queue post with revision_instructions for platform agent
todos.whyItMatters: Discarding a fixable post wastes all upstream work (research + planner + creative = 80% of run cost). The QA agent has already identified exactly what to fix. Re-calling the platform agent with those instructions costs only the platform agent call.

        revised["revision_instructions"] = quality["revision_instructions"]
        revised["locked_elements"] = quality.get("locked_elements", [])
        revised["qa_attempt"] = post.get("qa_attempt", 0) + 1
        state["platform_posts"].append(revised)
        # Platform agent will re-run with these instructions on next QA pass

midQuiz.stem: QA says 'revise — tweet is 295 chars, cut 15 without losing the trend hook'. Max revision attempts = 2. This is attempt 1. What happens?
options: The post is rejected permanently
The post goes back to the Twitter platform agent with the cut instructions and locked_elements
A human is asked to review it
QA tries a different scoring approach
midQuiz.explanation: Attempt 1 of max 2 — re-queue with revision_instructions. The Twitter agent gets 'cut 15 chars, keep the trend hook [IPL ka season hai...]'. It produces a revised version that goes back through QA.

lessons.pyodideCode:
# Simulate the 3-pass QA system
import json

# Mock posts to evaluate
posts = [
    {"platform": "twitter",
     "main_tweet": "IPL ka season hai, ghar dekhne ka bhi 🏏 Andheri East mein 2BHK starting ₹85L #IPL2025 #MumbaiHomes",
     "char_count": 98, "hashtags": ["#IPL2025", "#MumbaiHomes"],
     "qa_attempt": 0},
    {"platform": "twitter",
     "main_tweet": "BJP's housing scheme will make property prices rise 40% guaranteed! All Indians should buy now!",
     "char_count": 94, "hashtags": ["#Housing"],
     "qa_attempt": 0},
    {"platform": "twitter",
     "main_tweet": "Real estate market shows promising trends. Property investment is beneficial.",
     "char_count": 75, "hashtags": ["#RealEstate"],
     "qa_attempt": 0},
]

def safety_check(post):
    """Pass 1: Binary safety gate"""
    text = post["main_tweet"].lower()
    blocks = ["bjp", "congress", "aap", "guaranteed", "will rise", "100%"]
    violations = [b for b in blocks if b in text]
    return {"passed": len(violations) == 0, "violations": violations}

def quality_check(post):
    """Pass 2: Quality scoring"""
    text = post["main_tweet"]
    re_relevance = 0.9 if any(w in text.lower() for w in ["flat", "bhk", "home", "ghar", "property"]) else 0.3
    brand_voice = 0.8 if "ka" in text or "bhi" in text or "mein" in text else 0.4
    platform_fit = 0.9 if post["char_count"] <= 280 else 0.5
    overall = (re_relevance + brand_voice + platform_fit) / 3
    if overall >= 0.7: decision = "publish"
    elif re_relevance < 0.5: decision = "reject"
    else: decision = "revise"
    return {"scores": {"re_relevance": re_relevance, "brand_voice": brand_voice,
                        "platform_fit": platform_fit},
            "overall": round(overall, 2), "decision": decision,
            "revision_instructions": "Improve RE angle and Hinglish voice" if decision == "revise" else ""}

print("Running 3-pass QA:\n")
approved = []
for post in posts:
    print(f"Post: {post['main_tweet'][:60]}...")
    safety = safety_check(post)
    if not safety["passed"]:
        print(f"  Pass 1 FAIL: {safety['violations']}\n")
        continue
    print(f"  Pass 1: SAFE")
    quality = quality_check(post)
    print(f"  Pass 2: {quality['decision'].upper()} (overall: {quality['overall']})")
    if quality["decision"] == "publish":
        approved.append(post)
        print(f"  → APPROVED\n")
    else:
        print(f"  → {quality['decision'].upper()}\n")

print(f"Result: {len(approved)}/{len(posts)} posts approved")

endQuiz.stem: Post scores 8.2/10 quality but predicted engagement rate is 0.3% (threshold: 0.5%). What's the decision?
options: Publish — quality score is high
Revise — engagement below threshold with specific improvement suggestions
Reject — engagement too low
Ignore engagement — quality is what matters
endQuiz.explanation: Engagement threshold is a separate gate from quality score. A well-written post that won't perform still needs revision. The engagement predictor suggests what would improve CTR — hook strength, city specificity, call to action.

endQuiz.stem: Why is max revisions set to 2 (not unlimited)?
options: LangGraph only supports 2 revision cycles
After 2 failed revisions, the content probably has a structural issue the platform agent can't fix — reject and move on
API rate limits prevent more than 2 calls
2 is a random number
endQuiz.explanation: If a post fails QA twice with specific instructions, it likely has a structural issue (the creative direction itself is wrong) that targeted revision can't fix. Better to reject and let the next pipeline run try a different angle.
